Real Analysis

Existence of √2, Density, and Intervals

Existence of √2, Density, and Intervals

Existence of √2

The Archimedean property from the previous lecture showed that completeness has real teeth — it lets us prove facts about N\mathbb{N}N that field and order axioms alone cannot. We now put completeness to its most famous use: constructing a real number that Q\mathbb{Q}Q provably lacks. Recall from Lecture 2 that [[Irrationality of √2|no rational squares to 222]]; we now show a real number doing so does exist, and that it is unique.

Existence and Uniqueness of √2

There exists a unique x∈R≥0x \in \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}x∈R≥0​ such that x2=2x^2 = 2x2=2.

The proof strategy is a model for how completeness gets used throughout analysis: build the candidate as a supremum of a cleverly chosen set, then rule out every alternative using trichotomy.

Trichotomy (Recall)

For any a,b∈Ra,b \in \mathbb{R}a,b∈R, exactly one of the following holds: a<b,a=b,a>b.a<b,\quad a=b,\quad a>b.a<b,a=b,a>b.

Existence via the Completeness Axiom

Define S={ s∈R:s≥0 and s2<2 }.S = \{\, s \in \mathbb{R} : s \ge 0 \text{ and } s^2 < 2 \,\}.S={s∈R:s≥0 and s2<2}. Goal: show that x=sup⁡Sx = \sup Sx=supS exists and that x2=2x^2 = 2x2=2.

Step 1: SSS is nonempty and bounded above.

  • Nonempty: 0∈S0 \in S0∈S since 02=0<20^2 = 0 < 202=0<2. (Also 1∈S1 \in S1∈S, since 12=1<21^2 = 1 < 212=1<2.)
  • Bounded above: if s≥2s \ge 2s≥2, then s2≥4>2s^2 \ge 4 > 2s2≥4>2, contradicting s2<2s^2 < 2s2<2. Hence 222 is an upper bound.

By completeness, x=sup⁡Sx = \sup Sx=supS exists.

Step 2: Show x2=2x^2 = 2x2=2 by trichotomy. Exactly one of x2<2x^2 < 2x2<2, x2>2x^2 > 2x2>2, x2=2x^2 = 2x2=2 holds; we eliminate the first two, leaving only equality.

Case 1: Assume x2<2x^2 < 2x2<2 (contradiction)

We find n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N such that x+1n∈Sx + \tfrac{1}{n} \in Sx+n1​∈S, contradicting that xxx is an upper bound of SSS. Expand:

(x+1n)2=x2+2xn+1n2<x2+2xn+1n=x2+2x+1n.\left(x+\tfrac{1}{n}\right)^2 = x^2 + \tfrac{2x}{n} + \tfrac{1}{n^2} < x^2 + \tfrac{2x}{n} + \tfrac{1}{n} = x^2 + \tfrac{2x+1}{n}.(x+n1​)2=x2+n2x​+n21​<x2+n2x​+n1​=x2+n2x+1​.

Since 2−x2>02 - x^2 > 02−x2>0, the Archimedean property lets us choose n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N with n>2x+12−x2.n > \frac{2x+1}{2-x^2}.n>2−x22x+1​. Then (x+1n)2<2\left(x+\tfrac{1}{n}\right)^2 < 2(x+n1​)2<2, so x+1n∈Sx + \tfrac1n \in Sx+n1​∈S while x+1n>xx + \tfrac1n > xx+n1​>x — contradicting that xxx is an upper bound. Hence x2≮2x^2 \not< 2x2<2.

Case 2: Assume x2>2x^2 > 2x2>2 (contradiction)

We find n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N such that x−1nx - \tfrac1nx−n1​ is still an upper bound of SSS, contradicting the minimality of x=sup⁡Sx = \sup Sx=supS. Compute:

(x−1n)2=x2−2xn+1n2>x2−2xn.\left(x-\tfrac1n\right)^2 = x^2 - \tfrac{2x}{n} + \tfrac{1}{n^2} > x^2 - \tfrac{2x}{n}.(x−n1​)2=x2−n2x​+n21​>x2−n2x​.

Choose n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N with n>2xx2−2andn>1x.n > \frac{2x}{x^2-2} \quad\text{and}\quad n > \frac1x.n>x2−22x​andn>x1​. Then (x−1n)2>2\left(x - \tfrac1n\right)^2 > 2(x−n1​)2>2 and x−1n>0x - \tfrac1n > 0x−n1​>0. For any s∈Ss \in Ss∈S, s≥0s \ge 0s≥0 and s2<2<(x−1n)2s^2 < 2 < \left(x-\tfrac1n\right)^2s2<2<(x−n1​)2, so s<x−1ns < x - \tfrac1ns<x−n1​ by monotonicity of squaring on R≥0\mathbb{R}_{\ge0}R≥0​. Thus x−1nx - \tfrac1nx−n1​ is an upper bound of SSS smaller than xxx — contradicting that x=sup⁡Sx = \sup Sx=supS. Hence x2≯2x^2 \not> 2x2>2.

Since both strict inequalities are impossible, x2=2x^2 = 2x2=2.

Uniqueness

Suppose x,y∈R≥0x, y \in \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}x,y∈R≥0​ satisfy x2=y2=2x^2 = y^2 = 2x2=y2=2. Then (x−y)(x+y)=x2−y2=0.(x-y)(x+y) = x^2 - y^2 = 0.(x−y)(x+y)=x2−y2=0. By the Zero-Product Property (Lecture 2), x=yx = yx=y or x=−yx = -yx=−y. Since x,y≥0x, y \ge 0x,y≥0 and not both zero, x+y>0x + y > 0x+y>0, ruling out x=−yx=-yx=−y unless both vanish; either way x−y=0x - y = 0x−y=0, so x=yx = yx=y.

The unique nonnegative solution is denoted 2\sqrt{2}2​.

Where this leaves us

Combined with Lecture 2’s proof that 2∉Q\sqrt{2} \notin \mathbb{Q}2​∈/Q, we now have a genuine element of R∖Q\mathbb{R} \setminus \mathbb{Q}R∖Q, constructed entirely from the completeness axiom. This is the payoff promised back when irrationality of 2\sqrt22​ was first proved: R\mathbb{R}R really does fill in the gaps that Q\mathbb{Q}Q leaves behind.

Density Theorem

Knowing that both Q\mathbb{Q}Q and R∖Q\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}R∖Q are nonempty is one thing; we now show both are dense — arbitrarily close to every real number. The rational case leans on the Archimedean property, while the irrational case will lean on the rational case together with 2\sqrt22​ just constructed.

Density of the Rationals

Density of the Rationals

If x,y∈Rx, y \in \mathbb{R}x,y∈R with x<yx < yx<y, then there exists r∈Qr \in \mathbb{Q}r∈Q such that x<r<yx < r < yx<r<y.

The idea is to stretch the interval (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) by a large enough factor nnn that it has length greater than 111, guaranteeing an integer inside; then shrink back down by dividing by nnn.

Integer in a Long Interval

If α,β∈R\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{R}α,β∈R with β−α>1\beta - \alpha > 1β−α>1, then there exists m∈Zm \in \mathbb{Z}m∈Z with α<m<β\alpha < m < \betaα<m<β.

Proof

Let m=⌈α⌉m = \lceil \alpha \rceilm=⌈α⌉. Then m−1≤α<m≤α+1<βm - 1 \le \alpha < m \le \alpha + 1 < \betam−1≤α<m≤α+1<β, so α<m<β\alpha < m < \betaα<m<β.

Proof of Density of the Rationals

Given x<yx < yx<y, use the Archimedean property to choose n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N with n(y−x)>1.n(y-x) > 1.n(y−x)>1. Then ny−nx>1ny - nx > 1ny−nx>1, so by the lemma there exists m∈Zm \in \mathbb{Z}m∈Z with nx<m<ny.nx < m < ny.nx<m<ny. Dividing by nnn gives x<mn<y,x < \frac{m}{n} < y,x<nm​<y, and r=m/n∈Qr = m/n \in \mathbb{Q}r=m/n∈Q is the desired rational.

Density of the Irrationals

Density of the Irrationals

If x,y∈Rx, y \in \mathbb{R}x,y∈R with x<yx < yx<y, then there exists z∈R∖Qz \in \mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}z∈R∖Q such that x<z<yx < z < yx<z<y.

The strategy is to shrink the interval (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) by dividing through by 2\sqrt22​, find a rational inside the shrunken interval using the theorem just proved, then scale back up — the factor of 2\sqrt22​ is exactly what turns that rational into an irrational.

Rational Times Irrational Is Irrational

If r∈Q∖{0}r \in \mathbb{Q}\setminus\{0\}r∈Q∖{0} and s∈R∖Qs \in \mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}s∈R∖Q, then rs∉Qrs \notin \mathbb{Q}rs∈/Q.

Proof of Density of the Irrationals

Fix the irrational 2>0\sqrt2 > 02​>0 from the theorem above. Since 2>0\sqrt2 > 02​>0, dividing preserves order: x2<y2.\frac{x}{\sqrt2} < \frac{y}{\sqrt2}.2​x​<2​y​. By density of the rationals, there exists r∈Qr \in \mathbb{Q}r∈Q with x2<r<y2.\frac{x}{\sqrt2} < r < \frac{y}{\sqrt2}.2​x​<r<2​y​. Multiplying by 2\sqrt22​ gives x<r2<y.x < r\sqrt2 < y.x<r2​<y. If r≠0r \neq 0r=0, the lemma shows r2r\sqrt2r2​ is irrational, giving the desired z=r2z = r\sqrt2z=r2​. (If r=0r=0r=0 happened to be forced, density of the rationals lets us choose rrr elsewhere in the interval instead, since the interval (x/2,y/2)(x/\sqrt2, y/\sqrt2)(x/2​,y/2​) contains infinitely many rationals.)

The shared architecture of both density proofs

Both theorems follow the same two-step pattern: transform the problem (scale by nnn, or divide by 2\sqrt22​) into a setting where existence is easy to see, then transform back. Density of the irrationals in particular depends on density of the rationals — it is not an independent argument, but rather rationals viewed through a 2\sqrt22​-scaled lens.

Intervals

We close by giving a rigorous characterization of what an “interval” is, and showing that every interval must take one of the familiar forms (a,b)(a,b)(a,b), [a,b][a,b][a,b], etc. — using sup⁡\supsup and inf⁡\infinf to pin down the endpoints.

Interval Notation

(a,b),[a,b],[a,b),(a,b](a,b), \quad [a,b], \quad [a,b), \quad (a,b](a,b),[a,b],[a,b),(a,b]

Interval (Convex Subset of R\mathbb{R}R)

A set S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R is an interval if for all x,y∈Sx, y \in Sx,y∈S with x<yx < yx<y, we have [x,y]⊆S.[x,y] \subseteq S.[x,y]⊆S.

This definition says nothing about boundedness or endpoints directly — it’s purely a “no gaps” condition. The next theorem shows that this condition, together with completeness, forces every interval into one of four familiar shapes, depending on whether it is bounded above, below, both, or neither.

Interval Classification by Bounds

Let S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R be a nonempty interval.

  • Case 1: SSS is bounded above and below. Let a=inf⁡Sa = \inf Sa=infS, b=sup⁡Sb = \sup Sb=supS. Then (a,b)⊆S⊆[a,b],(a,b) \subseteq S \subseteq [a,b],(a,b)⊆S⊆[a,b], so SSS is one of (a,b), [a,b), (a,b], [a,b](a,b),\ [a,b),\ (a,b],\ [a,b](a,b), [a,b), (a,b], [a,b], depending on endpoint membership.
  • Case 2: SSS is bounded above but not below. Let b=sup⁡Sb = \sup Sb=supS. Then SSS is one of (−∞,b), (−∞,b](-\infty,b),\ (-\infty,b](−∞,b), (−∞,b].
  • Case 3: SSS is bounded below but not above. Let a=inf⁡Sa = \inf Sa=infS. Then SSS is one of (a,∞), [a,∞)(a,\infty),\ [a,\infty)(a,∞), [a,∞).
  • Case 4: SSS is unbounded above and below. Then S=RS = \mathbb{R}S=R.

The heart of the theorem is Case 1; the remaining cases are proved the same way, simply omitting whichever bound doesn’t exist.

Case 1: Bounded Above and Below

Let a=inf⁡Sa = \inf Sa=infS, b=sup⁡Sb = \sup Sb=supS.

S⊆[a,b]S \subseteq [a,b]S⊆[a,b]: for any s∈Ss \in Ss∈S, a≤s≤ba \le s \le ba≤s≤b directly from the definitions of infimum and supremum.

(a,b)⊆S(a,b) \subseteq S(a,b)⊆S: fix y∈(a,b)y \in (a,b)y∈(a,b). Since y<b=sup⁡Sy < b = \sup Sy<b=supS, yyy is not an upper bound of SSS, so there exists s∈Ss \in Ss∈S with y<sy < sy<s. Since a<ya < ya<y, yyy is not a lower bound of SSS either, so there exists s′∈Ss' \in Ss′∈S with s′<ys' < ys′<y. Since SSS is an interval and s′<y<ss' < y < ss′<y<s, we get [s′,s]⊆S[s',s] \subseteq S[s′,s]⊆S, hence y∈Sy \in Sy∈S.

Combining both inclusions, (a,b)⊆S⊆[a,b](a,b) \subseteq S \subseteq [a,b](a,b)⊆S⊆[a,b]; the exact form among (a,b),[a,b),(a,b],[a,b](a,b), [a,b), (a,b], [a,b](a,b),[a,b),(a,b],[a,b] is then determined by whether aaa and/or bbb themselves belong to SSS.

Summary of the Remaining Cases
  • Case 2: if SSS has sup⁡S=b\sup S = bsupS=b and no infimum in R\mathbb{R}R, then S=(−∞,b)S = (-\infty,b)S=(−∞,b) or S=(−∞,b]S=(-\infty,b]S=(−∞,b].
  • Case 3: if SSS has inf⁡S=a\inf S = ainfS=a and no supremum in R\mathbb{R}R, then S=(a,∞)S = (a,\infty)S=(a,∞) or S=[a,∞)S=[a,\infty)S=[a,∞).
  • Case 4: if SSS has neither an infimum nor a supremum in R\mathbb{R}R, then S=RS = \mathbb{R}S=R.
Why unboundedness forces S=RS = \mathbb{R}S=R

Case 4 is really Case 1 pushed to its limit: with no finite aaa or bbb to bound the “gap-free” region, the same argument used to show (a,b)⊆S(a,b)\subseteq S(a,b)⊆S runs for every pair of reals, so SSS must contain all of R\mathbb{R}R.
